symbols.name: \(O_i\)
symbols.def: Observed right-censored single-initiation illness-death trajectory of individual \(i\).

symbols.name: \(P_{\mathrm{full}}\)
symbols.def: A full-filtration causal-law extension having observed-data margin \(P\).

symbols.name: \(\mathfrak E(P)\)
symbols.def: Compatible full-data extensions of the observed law \(P\).

symbols.name: \(u\)
symbols.def: A deterministic eligible initiation time in the interior localization window.

symbols.name: \(T^u\)
symbols.def: Terminal-event time under no initiation before \(u\) and initiation at \(u\) if eligible.

symbols.name: \(Y_0(t)\)
symbols.def: Untreated, alive, uncensored eligibility indicator immediately before \(t\).

symbols.name: \(\alpha_{01,P}\)
symbols.def: Observed natural initiation hazard while eligible.

symbols.name: \(\alpha_{12,P}\)
symbols.def: Observed post-initiation terminal-event hazard with arguments \(t,u,x\).

symbols.name: \(\alpha_{C1,P}\)
symbols.def: Observed post-initiation censoring hazard with arguments \(t,u,x\).

symbols.name: \(f_{U,P}\)
symbols.def: Natural initiation density among trajectories eligible at initiation.

symbols.name: \(f_-\)
symbols.def: Local lower bound on the initiation density.

symbols.name: \(b_n\)
symbols.def: Smallest bandwidth in the indexed family, \(b_n=\inf_{c\in\mathcal C}b(c)\).

symbols.name: \(q_b\)
symbols.def: Normalized nonnegative initiation-time density at width \(b\).

symbols.name: \(\overline Q_b\)
symbols.def: No-draw survival mass of the stochastic initiation rule.

symbols.name: \(\Psi_u(P)\)
symbols.def: Observed-law product-integral terminal-risk functional under deterministic initiation at \(u\).

symbols.name: \(g_P\)
symbols.def: Unique Hölder version of the initiation-time response.

symbols.name: \(\theta(P)\)
symbols.def: Hölder-selected exact-time terminal-event risk, invariant across compatible full-data extensions through their almost-everywhere initiation-time responses.

symbols.name: \(\theta_b(P)\)
symbols.def: Risk under the localized stochastic initiation intervention.

symbols.name: \(\phi_{P,b}(\cdot;c)\)
symbols.def: Martinussen--Knudsen--Rytgaard efficient influence function for \(\theta_{b(c)}(P)\), with all five observed hazard and censoring nuisance arguments displayed in its construction.

symbols.name: \(\widehat s_n(c)\)
symbols.def: Estimated standard error based on the cross-fitted score.

symbols.name: \(\mathcal C\)
symbols.def: Index set for simultaneous bandwidth or target-time analysis.

symbols.name: \(d_{P,n}\)
symbols.def: Canonical covariance semimetric of the standardized EIF class.

symbols.name: \(q\)
symbols.def: Moment exponent in the CCK predicate.

symbols.name: \(B_n\)
symbols.def: Envelope moment bound in the CCK predicate.

symbols.name: \(\Delta_{\mathrm{CCK},n}\)
symbols.def: Displayed CCK Gaussian-coupling error expression at effective count \(m_n\).

symbols.name: \(\mathbb G_n\)
symbols.def: Centered Gaussian process with the canonical covariance of \(\mathcal F_n\).

symbols.name: \(\mathbb Q_{P,\lambda}\)
symbols.def: Candidate centered marked compound-Poisson critical-regime law.

symbols.name: \(\mathcal P_s(L)\)
symbols.def: Fixed observed-law model for exact-time honest inference.

symbols.name: \(D_n(C_n)\)
symbols.def: Worst-case expected diameter of a confidence procedure.

symbols.name: \(C^{\mathrm{BA}}_{n,\alpha}[b_n]\)
symbols.def: Candidate bias-aware exact-target interval associated with the bandwidth sequence \(b_n\).

symbols.name: \(\mathcal B^G_\alpha\)
symbols.def: Bandwidth sequences whose candidate bias-aware interval is honest in the Gaussian effective-count regime.

assumptions.condition: Under \(P_{\mathrm{full}}\), \(T=T^U\) almost surely when natural initiation occurs while eligible and uncensored.

For every \(u\), under \(P_{\mathrm{full}}\) the full-filtration intervention that suppresses initiation before \(u\) and initiates at \(u\) when eligible has terminal-event intensities \(\alpha_{02,P}(t\mid X)\) before \(u\) and \(\alpha_{12,P}(t\mid u,X)\) after \(u\).

novel.justification: This is the observed-hazard causal-validity premise stated as necessary in the illness-death intervention framework. It is credible when clinical initiation decisions and post-initiation prognosis are fully represented by the recorded longitudinal history, but the correspondence between the full-filtration causal intervention and the feasible observed-filtration intervention is an identifying restriction, not a consequence of observed data.

The observed law must admit at least one full-data extension satisfying the stated consistency, observed-hazard causal-validity, and CAR restrictions. This explicit existential restriction is credible for a longitudinally measured illness-death cohort only when the recorded history is adequate for those causal and coarsening restrictions; it does not select a unique unobserved extension.

This is an uncited constructed submodel restriction for lower-bound and critical-regime constructions. It is feasible in a cohort administratively observed through \(t_0\), and it remains within the bounded-hazard scope.

definitions.name: \(\mathcal H_s(L)\)
definitions.construction: \(\mathcal H_s(L)=\{h:[u_\star-\delta,u_\star+\delta]\to\mathbb R:|h(x)-h(y)|\le L|x-y|^s\text{ for }0<s\le1;\ h\in C^1,\ |h'(x)-h'(y)|\le L|x-y|^{s-1}\text{ for }1<s\le2\}\).

definitions.name: \(\Psi_u(P)\)
definitions.construction: \(\Psi_u(P)=1-E_P[\exp\{-\int_0^u\alpha_{02,P}(r\mid X)dr-\int_u^{t_0}\alpha_{12,P}(r\mid u,X)dr\}]\) for \(u\in[u_\star-\delta,u_\star+\delta]\). This product integral suppresses natural initiation before \(u\) and uses the initiation-time-specific post-initiation hazard after \(u\).

definitions.name: \(q_b,a_b,\overline Q_b,\theta_b(P)\)
definitions.construction: \(q_b(u)=b^{-1}K((u-u_\star)/b)\mathbf 1\{|u-u_\star|\le b\},\quad\overline Q_b(t)=1-\int_0^tq_b(r)dr,\quad a_b(t)=q_b(t)/\overline Q_b(t)\) when \(\overline Q_b(t)>0\). While \(Y_0(t)=1\), the observed-filtration intervention replaces \(\alpha_{01,P}(t\mid X)\) by \(a_b(t)\), so it has support \([u_\star-b,u_\star+b]\), conditional draw density \(q_b\), no-draw mass \(\overline Q_b(t)\), and normalization \(\int q_b=1\). It is dominated on its support by \(q_b/f_{U,P}\le\|K\|_\infty/(bf_-)\). Finally, \(\theta_b(P)=\int q_b(u)\Psi_u(P)du\).

definitions.name: \(\phi_{P,b}(\cdot;c)\)
definitions.construction: \(\phi_{P,b}(O;c)=\phi^{\mathrm{MKR}}(O;\alpha_{01,P},\alpha_{02,P},\alpha_{12,P},\alpha_{C0,P},\alpha_{C1,P},q_{b(c)})\), where \(\phi^{\mathrm{MKR}}\) is the mean-zero efficient influence-function formula for the covariate-dependent observed-filtration stochastic hazard intervention in Martinussen--Knudsen--Rytgaard (2026, Theorem 4.1); their Theorem 3.1 is its no-covariate building block.

definitions.name: \(\widehat\theta_b(c),\widehat\phi_c,R_{1n}(c),R_{2n}(c)\)
definitions.construction: For \(i\in I_k\), set \(\widehat\phi_c(O_i)=\phi_{\widehat P^{(-k)},b(c)}(O_i;c)\) and \(\widehat\theta_b(c)=K_0^{-1}\sum_{k=1}^{K_0}\{\theta_{b(c)}(\widehat P^{(-k)})+|I_k|^{-1}\sum_{i\in I_k}\widehat\phi_c(O_i)\}\). Define \(R_{1n}(c)=(P_n-P)\{\widehat\phi_c-\phi_{P,b}(\cdot;c)\}\) and \(R_{2n}(c)=\widehat\theta_b(c)-\theta_{b(c)}(P)-(P_n-P)\phi_{P,b}(\cdot;c)-R_{1n}(c)\), yielding \(\widehat\theta_b(c)-\theta_{b(c)}(P)=(P_n-P)\phi_{P,b}(\cdot;c)+R_{1n}(c)+R_{2n}(c)\).

definitions.name: Localized nuisance norms and remainder targets
definitions.construction: For any nuisance error \(r\), set \(\|r\|_{P,b(c)}^2=E_P[\int_0^{t_0}Y_0(t)q_{b(c)}(t)r(t,X)^2\{f_{U,P}(t)\}^{-1}dt]\). The handle is to bound separately \(\|\widehat\alpha_{01}-\alpha_{01,P}\|_{P,b(c)}\), \(\|\widehat\alpha_{02}-\alpha_{02,P}\|_{P,b(c)}\), \(\|\widehat\alpha_{12}-\alpha_{12,P}\|_{P,b(c)}\), \(\|\widehat\alpha_{C0}-\alpha_{C0,P}\|_{P,b(c)}\), \(\|\widehat\alpha_{C1}-\alpha_{C1,P}\|_{P,b(c)}\), the corresponding conditional-survival errors, and the score error \(\|\widehat\phi_c-\phi_{P,b}(\cdot;c)\|_{P,2}\), then calculate each pairwise product contributing to \(R_{2n}(c)\) and the cross-fitted empirical term \(R_{1n}(c)\) at scale \(s_{P,n}(c)\).

definitions.name: \(\mathcal F_n,d_{P,n},\Delta_{\mathrm{CCK},n}\)
definitions.construction: \(\mathcal F_n=\{\sqrt{b(c)}\phi_{P,b(c)}(\cdot;c)/\sqrt{b(c)v_{P,b}(c)}:c\in\mathcal C\}\), \(d_{P,n}(c,c')=\{E_P[f_c(O)-f_{c'}(O)]^2\}^{1/2}\) for its displayed elements \(f_c,f_{c'}\), and \(\Delta_{\mathrm{CCK},n}=\{B_nK_n^2/m_n^{1/2-1/q}+B_n^2K_n^3/m_n^{1-2/q}\}^{1/3}\), where \(K_n=\log(A_n\vee n)\) and \(m_n=n\inf_{c\in\mathcal C}b(c)=nb_n\). The handle is to verify \(E_PF_n^q\le B_n^q\), \(\sup_Q\log N(\epsilon\|F_n\|_{Q,2},\mathcal F_n,L_2(Q))\le v_n\log(A_n/\epsilon)\), nondegenerate variance, covariance-estimation consistency, Gaussian sample boundedness and \(d_{P,n}\)-equicontinuity, and the anti-concentration bound \(\sup_zP(|\sup_{c\in\mathcal C}|\mathbb G_n(c)|-z|\le\varepsilon)\le C\varepsilon\{E\sup_c|\mathbb G_n(c)|+1\}\).

definitions.name: \(\mathbb Q_{P,\lambda}\)
definitions.construction: Handle: construct a triangular no-censoring bounded-hazard subclass and use Laplace functionals of initiation points in \([u_\star-b_n,u_\star+b_n]\) and their EIF marks. The candidate law is a centered marked compound-Poisson law with intensity, mark distribution, compensator, and any atom derived by that construction; \(\mathbb Q_{P,\lambda}\) names this candidate law only.

definitions.name: \(\mathcal P_s(L)\)
definitions.construction: \(\mathcal P_s(L)=\{P\in\mathcal P(\mathcal O):\exists P_{\mathrm{full}}\in\mathfrak E(P),\ P\text{ satisfies }\mathrm{ass:causal-positivity},\mathrm{ass:bounded-hazards},\mathrm{ass:local-density},\mathrm{ass:interior-preoutcome},\mathrm{ass:holder-version},\mathrm{ass:kernel}\}\).

definitions.name: \(\mathcal P_s^0(L)\)
definitions.construction: \(\mathcal P_s^0(L)=\{P\in\mathcal P(\mathcal O):\exists P_{\mathrm{full}}\in\mathfrak E(P),\ P\text{ satisfies }\mathrm{ass:causal-positivity},\mathrm{ass:bounded-hazards},\mathrm{ass:local-density},\mathrm{ass:interior-preoutcome},\mathrm{ass:holder-version},\mathrm{ass:kernel},\mathrm{ass:no-censoring-subclass}\}\).

definitions.name: \(B_s(b),C^{\mathrm{BA}}_{n,\alpha}[b_n],\mathcal B^G_\alpha\)
definitions.construction: Let \(\mu_s(K)=\int|v|^sK(v)dv\) and \(\kappa_s=1\) for \(0<s\le1\), \(\kappa_s=1/s\) for \(1<s\le2\). Set \(B_s(b)=\kappa_sLb^s\mu_s(K)\). For each bandwidth sequence \(b_n\), instantiate \(\mathcal C=\{0\}\) and \(b(0)=b_n\) in the cross-fitted construction, and set \(C^{\mathrm{BA}}_{n,\alpha}[b_n]=[\widehat\theta_{b_n}(0)-\widehat s_n(0)\{z_{1-\alpha/2}+B_s(b_n)/\widehat s_n(0)\},\widehat\theta_{b_n}(0)+\widehat s_n(0)\{z_{1-\alpha/2}+B_s(b_n)/\widehat s_n(0)\}]\). Then \(\mathcal B^G_\alpha=\{(b_n):b_n\downarrow0,nb_n\to\infty,C^{\mathrm{BA}}_{n,\alpha}[b_n]\in\mathcal C_{n,\alpha}\}\).

statements.statement: For every \(P_{\mathrm{full}}\in\mathfrak E(P)\), \(P_{\mathrm{full}}(T^u\le t_0)=\Psi_u(P)\) for \(f_{U,P}\)-almost every \(u\in[u_\star-\delta,u_\star+\delta]\).
statements.justification: The proposition makes the full-filtration causal intervention and its observed-filtration representation explicit for each compatible extension.
statements.gap: Martinussen--Knudsen--Rytgaard (2026, Abstract) state that correspondence requires conditions; this is a source-faithful identification substrate, not a claimed advance.
statements.consumer: It assigns causal meaning to the exact initiation-time terminal-risk contrast without choosing an unobserved extension.

statements.statement: Under local density positivity and the Hölder-version restriction, \([u\mapsto\Psi_u(P)]_{f_{U,P}}\cap\mathcal H_s(L)\) contains exactly one member. Hence \(\theta(P)=g_P(u_\star)\) is an observed-law functional.
statements.justification: A continuous version upgrades almost-everywhere identification to a point target.
statements.gap: This is standard point-target well-posedness rather than a new estimability claim.
statements.consumer: It makes exact-time loss and honest coverage well defined.

statements.statement: For every \(P\in\mathcal P_s(L)\) and every \(P_{\mathrm{full}}\in\mathfrak E(P)\), \(P_{\mathrm{full}}(T^u\le t_0)=\Psi_u(P)\) for \(f_{U,P}\)-almost every \(u\). Thus every compatible extension has the same Hölder-selected version \(g_P\), and the extension-invariant observed-law target is \(\theta(P)=g_P(u_\star)\).
statements.justification: The lemma separates existence of a compatible causal extension from invariance of the observed-law point target.
statements.gap: The compatibility qualification makes explicit the full-versus-observed distinction in Martinussen--Knudsen--Rytgaard (2026, Abstract); it is identification substrate rather than a new statistical claim.
statements.consumer: It makes the causal headline, loss, and confidence procedures invariant to the unobserved full-data extension.

statements.statement: The at-risk hazard \(a_b\) implements the nonnegative normalized density \(q_b\), and its identified terminal risk is \(\theta_b(P)=\int q_b(u)g_P(u)du\). Under CAR, pathwise linearity of the stochastic hazard-intervention functional implies that \(\phi_{P,b}(\cdot;c)\) in \(\mathrm{def:eif}\) is its EIF, not merely a score obtained by substitution.
statements.justification: This separates a legitimate stochastic intervention from a signed equivalent kernel and fixes the score target.
statements.gap: Martinussen--Knudsen--Rytgaard (2026, Abstract) derive an EIF for a smoothed stochastic intervention; the proposition verifies compatibility with the displayed hazard rule.
statements.consumer: It licenses the local EIF used in the phase question.

statements.statement: For \(P\in\mathcal P_s(L)\), \(|\theta_b(P)-\theta(P)|\le B_s(b)\). If \(g_P\) is constant on \([u_\star-b,u_\star+b]\), then \(\theta_b(P)-\theta(P)=0\). For fixed \(b>0\), the intervention and EIF reduce to the regular smoothed-intervention setting of Martinussen--Knudsen--Rytgaard.
statements.justification: The exact Hölder envelope distinguishes worst-case approximation from actual bias and supplies the required regular reduction.
statements.gap: The bias algebra is standard in Armstrong--Kolesár (2020, Sections 2--3); the fixed-window reduction is a compatibility check, not a strict extension.
statements.consumer: It supplies the deterministic bias component of a candidate exact-target interval.

statements.statement: For \(m_n=nb_n\), derive the effective-count phase transition from primitive calculations. When \(m_n\to\infty\), can the actual localized norms in \(\mathrm{def:localized-remainders}\) yield \(\sup_{c\in\mathcal C}|R_{1n}(c)+R_{2n}(c)|/s_{P,n}(c)\to_P0\), \(\sup_c|\widehat s_n(c)/s_{P,n}(c)-1|\to_P0\), \(v_{P,b}(c)\asymp b(c)^{-1}\), and a pointwise, or where supported uniform, Gaussian approximation by verifying the displayed CCK predicates and \(\Delta_{\mathrm{CCK},n}\to0\)? When \(m_n\to\lambda\in(0,\infty)\), can an explicit nonempty CAR-preserving bounded-hazard triangular subclass of \(\mathcal P_s^0(L)\) be constructed whose localized initiation point process and marks converge by Laplace functionals to \(\mathbb Q_{P,\lambda}\), with identified intensity, mark distribution, compensator, atom, vanishing nonlocal EIF and nuisance terms, and a non-Gaussian limit?
statements.justification: The local marked counting process may change the limit experiment at bounded effective count.
statements.gap: Martinussen--Knudsen--Rytgaard (2026, Abstract) stop at regular smoothing; Schindl--Shen--Kennedy (2026, Abstract) derive effective-sample-size behavior for static continuous exposures; CCK (2014, Theorem 2.1) gives a Gaussian tool but no complementary CAR multistate rare-event experiment.
statements.consumer: It determines whether a shrinking-window survival estimate supports Gaussian calibration or needs a critical-regime likelihood analysis.

statements.statement: Determine whether \(\mathcal B^G_\alpha\) is nonempty, its boundary, and \(\inf_{(b_n)\in\mathcal B^G_\alpha}D_n(C^{\mathrm{BA}}_{n,\alpha}[b_n])\) relative to \(D^\star_{n,\alpha}\). Prove any lower bound through an explicit modulus or testing construction over \(\mathcal P_s^0(L)\), and characterize the replacement for this Gaussian bandwidth set when \(nb_n=O(1)\).
statements.justification: The object gives exact-target honest inference a defined coverage class, loss, benchmark, and candidate procedure.
statements.gap: Armstrong--Kolesár (2020, Sections 2--4) give worst-case-bias intervals in local regression, while Ai--Huang--Zhang (2026, Abstract) give uniform continuous-treatment bands; neither handles a learned CAR illness-death EIF or a bounded-count compound-Poisson experiment.
statements.consumer: It sets a formally auditable window-selection and uncertainty-reporting rule for the 1,896-couple IUI delay analysis.

sampling_model.design: Independent sampling from an observed right-censored single-initiation illness-death law \(P\) admitting a compatible full-data extension in \(\mathfrak E(P)\).
sampling_model.units: Each trajectory records baseline covariates, eligibility, initiation, terminal event, and censoring.
sampling_model.treatment: The observed-filtration regularization replaces the at-risk initiation hazard by \(a_b=q_b/\overline Q_b\). Each compatible full-filtration extension satisfies the stated causal-validity restriction, and the Hölder-selected target is invariant across those extensions.
sampling_model.overlap: Causal initiation positivity and local initiation-density positivity hold on an interior pre-outcome window.
sampling_model.nuisance_estimation: The Gaussian question conditions on displayed componentwise localized remainder calculations; fully adaptive delayed-entry survival learning is unresolved.

tldr: The focal object is the effective-count phase transition of the CAR-censored illness-death EIF for exact initiation time. The observed law is required to admit compatible full-data extensions, and settled identification and Hölder selection make \(\theta(P)=g_P(u_\star)\) the same selected point target for every such extension's almost-everywhere causal response. Further settled propositions define a legitimate nonnegative stochastic hazard intervention, bridge it to the published EIF, and bound its approximation bias. The open work is to derive, rather than assume, a Gaussian regime when \(nb_n\to\infty\), construct the bounded-count marked compound-Poisson experiment when \(nb_n\to\lambda\), and determine the consequent honest exact-target diameter frontier.

project_justification.gap: Fixed-window stochastic-intervention survival theory and continuous-treatment localization theory do not determine the limiting experiment of a learned CAR-censored illness-death EIF when the local initiation count is bounded.
project_justification.niche: Initiation time is a continuous treatment embedded in a multistate counting process: compatible full-data extensions yield an extension-invariant observed-law target, while localization raises the EIF scale and CAR weighting and nuisance learning create terms absent from static dose-response experiments.
project_justification.fill: The proposal fixes the compatibility-qualified causal and statistical objects, then poses the Gaussian-to-marked-Poisson construction and the associated honest-inference modulus as the theorem-level open content.

related_work: Martinussen, Knudsen, and Rytgaard (2026, Abstract and Theorem 4.1) distinguish feasible observed-data and causally valid interventions, explain fixed-time nonregularity, and derive the covariate-dependent smoothed-intervention EIF; their Theorem 3.1 is the no-covariate building block. Their paper does not state a vanishing-window bounded-effective-count marked-Poisson limit. Schindl, Shen, and Kennedy (2026, Abstract) derive effective-sample-size bounds for exponentially tilted static continuous exposures, without censoring or illness-death transition hazards. Kennedy, Ma, McHugh, and Small (2017, Sections 2--3) develop kernel-smoothed doubly robust continuous-treatment curves, and Colangelo and Lee (2026, Sections 2--4) develop localized DML, but neither has multistate CAR scores. Bonvini and Kennedy (2026, Abstract) analyze dose-response rates through higher-order methods, while Kennedy, Balakrishnan, Robins, and Wasserman (2024, Sections 2--4) develop localized Hölder point-target minimax tools; neither considers a time-dependent survival intervention. Westling, Luedtke, Gilbert, and Carone (2024, Abstract) establish machine-learning survival inference for baseline treatment rather than a shrinking initiation hazard. Chernozhukov, Chetverikov, and Kato (2014, Theorem 2.1) provide the changing-class Gaussian coupling predicate, not a rare-event complement. Armstrong and Kolesár (2020, Sections 2--4) establish worst-case-bias honest intervals in local regression. Ai, Huang, and Zhang (2026, Abstract) establish a uniform Bahadur representation, bootstrap bands, and data-driven bias control for ordinary continuous-treatment response processes, but do not treat a CAR-censored illness-death EIF with learned multistate hazards or construct a bounded-effective-count marked compound-Poisson experiment.
technical_internal_limitation: The candidate critical law is not a consequence of \(nb_n\to\lambda\) or of its handle. It requires a separately verified CAR-preserving triangular construction, and no conclusion is asserted for an estimator that deliberately borrows information outside the localized-EIF regime.
honest_scope: The settled substrate is extension-invariant identification for every compatible full-data extension under the stated causal-validity restriction, Hölder point-target selection, a nonnegative observed-filtration intervention, its EIF bridge, the bias envelope, and fixed-window reduction. Gaussian approximation, compound-Poisson convergence, uniformity, nuisance feasibility, confidence-set honesty, and optimal diameter remain open. The IUI application is an applied consumer only and does not assert exact-day causal identification or valid inference absent the stated assumptions and open results.

bibliography.citation: T. Martinussen, M. B. Knudsen, and H. Rytgaard. Debiased inference for stochastic treatment interventions with survival outcomes. arXiv:2605.31130, 2026.

E. H. Kennedy, Z. Ma, M. D. McHugh, and D. S. Small. Non-parametric methods for doubly robust estimation of continuous treatment effects. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society Series B, 79(4), 2017.
K. Colangelo and Y.-Y. Lee. Double debiased machine learning nonparametric inference with continuous treatments. Journal of Business and Economic Statistics, 44(1), 2026.
M. Bonvini and E. H. Kennedy. Fast convergence rates for dose-response estimation. arXiv:2207.11825, 2026.
E. H. Kennedy, S. Balakrishnan, J. M. Robins, and L. Wasserman. Minimax rates for heterogeneous causal effect estimation. Annals of Statistics, 52(2), 2024.
K. Schindl, S. Shen, and E. H. Kennedy. Incremental effects for continuous exposures. arXiv:2409.11967, 2026.
T. Westling, A. Luedtke, P. B. Gilbert, and M. Carone. Inference for treatment-specific survival curves using machine learning. Journal of the American Statistical Association, 119, 2024.
V. Chernozhukov, D. Chetverikov, and K. Kato. Gaussian approximation of suprema of empirical processes. Annals of Statistics, 42(4), 2014.
T. B. Armstrong and M. Kolesar. Simple and honest confidence intervals in nonparametric regression. Quantitative Economics, 11(1), 2020.
C. Ai, W. Huang, and Z. Zhang. Data-Driven Uniform Inference for General Continuous Treatment Models via Minimum-Variance Weighting. arXiv:2602.01595, 2026.

comparator_promise_table.comparator_claim: A fixed smoothed stochastic survival intervention has an EIF, while a fixed initiation-time target is non-pathwise differentiable.
Static continuous-exposure incremental effects have effective-sample-size-dependent bounds.
Kernel-smoothed doubly robust methods estimate continuous-treatment response curves.
Localized cross-fitted DML supports continuous-treatment inference under nuisance conditions.
Higher-order methods give smoothness-sensitive dose-response risk bounds.
A Hölder causal point target admits localized minimax analysis.
Cross-fitted estimators provide baseline-treatment survival-curve inference.
Empirical-process suprema admit Gaussian approximation under moment and entropy conditions.
Worst-case smoothness bias yields honest nonparametric intervals and length comparisons.
Weighted local-linear continuous-treatment estimators have uniform Bahadur representations and data-driven bias-control bands.
